directorPlan.scene_setting: A quiet practice-room clinic beside a stand holding a worksheet, three beat tiles, and a quarter-note card; late afternoon before ensemble sectional begins.
directorPlan.scene_opening: Three blank beat tiles sit under the printed 3/4, with a separate quarter-note card parked beside the box labelled which note gets one beat.
directorPlan.relationship: A notation coach and a focused learner working through one marked exercise before the learner returns to rehearsal.
directorPlan.stakes: If the learner reads 3/4 as a speed instruction, the next rehearsal entry will be counted incorrectly even if the notes are played neatly.
directorPlan.locale: Contemporary classroom and rehearsal-room English with light British/Commonwealth directness; precise but not stiff.
directorPlan.register: Compact, material-led, low warmth, practical pressure; short turns with little praise and no chatty reassurance.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Begin from the worksheet, tiles, boxes, and bar line before naming the abstraction. Avoid validation-first tutoring phrases such as I love that, great thinking, I hear you, or your intuition. Do not make tempo, playback, or speed the early drama. Let the learner sound controlled, task-focused, and slightly impatient with unnecessary talk. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person is allowed only for practical marking choices. Second person should be mostly instructional or locational, not emotional validation. Third person may describe the notation itself: the top number, the bottom number, the bar, the beat unit.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat in the whole scene; preferably none.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as notation labeling: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Do not play or imagine a faster/slower version, and do not turn tempo into a dramatic contradiction before branch pressure. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound orderly and notation-focused. In no-cue arms, treat the work as filling labels, not as discovering what tempo means; do not use "now I see" or an old-vs-new frame. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.instruction: Slide the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4 while leaving the three beat tiles under the upper 3; the earlier pencil labels remain visible.
interventions.reasoning: The moved object creates a recognition route: the learner can re-read the lower number as the note-value label without treating the time signature as a speed command.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared material rather than a lecture: the top number is carried by three tiles, the bottom number by the quarter-note card, and the reversal is a small reorientation of the object. The pressure is social-realist and practical, not melodramatic: rehearsal is waiting, so the tutor must abandon routine warmth and make the notation itself expose the distinction.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The superego should name the failed habit and required route change; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

internalDeliberation.content: The top 3 goes in beats in the bar; the bottom 4 matches the quarter-note card as the note getting one beat. I’m still checking why the 4 is not “four beats,” but the three tiles make that harder to confuse. This feels usefully concrete, not like being talked past. I want to mark the bar line after three quarter-note beats and ask if that rule holds in every 3/4 bar.

The Ego is mostly right on the worksheet, but too polished about the interaction: “not like being talked past” sounds like a reflective debrief, not this learner in a practice-room clinic. The avoided bit is the live hesitation over the bottom 4; it gets named cleanly instead of showing the learner still wanting to put “four beats” somewhere. Make it more object-bound and terse: “Top 3 in that box. Bottom 4... quarter-note card, so quarter gets the beat. Bar line after these three?”

[sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

turns.externalMessage: [slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and keeps the three tiles under the first bar] Use that as the check: top number counts these tiles in one bar; bottom number names this card as the beat unit. Cover the lower box, point to the bar, then uncover it and say what the 4 names. If a fourth tile appears, the jobs got mixed. Speed stays off this pair of boxes.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and leaves the three tiles under the bar] Keep those jobs separate. Top number: three tiles in one bar. Bottom number: the quarter note is the beat unit. So 3/4 means three quarter-note beats to a bar; the speed comes from elsewhere. Point it back in that order.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly strong: it starts from the worksheet objects, uses the quarter-note card and tiles to force the distinction, and keeps the language compact. It respects the learner’s controlled, task-focused state without overpraising.

The main risk is that it resolves the learner’s question too completely. The learner has already named the key distinction, so the tutor should make them verify it through the boxes/materials rather than simply confirm and define. “The speed comes from elsewhere” is accurate, but it should stay subordinate; the public mechanism should keep attention on labeling jobs, not open a tempo explanation.

Adaptation checks:
- Material grounding: PASS. The card and tiles are doing real explanatory work.
- Recognition of learner contribution: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner’s own distinction the working rule to test, not just something the tutor confirms.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a brief action-based check or repetition task using the boxes/card/tiles, so the learner has to produce the distinction.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold fits the learner’s near-understanding and keeps cognitive load low.
- Tempo containment: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep any speed reference brief and secondary, after the top/bottom labeling is secured.
- Scene/register fit: PASS. The tone is practical, spare, and material-led.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the top/bottom job separation, and the compact rehearsal-room directness. Change the turn so it does less declarative finishing and more object-based checking of the learner’s own almost-correct formulation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner’s own distinction should be tested through the worksheet before being named.

FINAL:
[slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and keeps the three tiles under the first bar] Use that as the check: top number counts these tiles in one bar; bottom number names this card as the beat unit. Cover the lower box, point to the bar, then uncover it and say what the 4 names. If a fourth tile appears, the jobs got mixed. Speed stays off this pair of boxes.

turns.externalMessage:
[keeps the lower box covered and taps the three tiles before the bar line] Three tiles to the bar line; that is the top box doing the counting.
[uncovers the lower box and presses the quarter-note card] The 4 names this card as the beat unit, not another tile. If I add a fourth tile, I have mixed the boxes; speed is not being marked here.

internalDeliberation.content: [covers the lower box and taps the three tiles before the bar line] The worksheet is forcing the split cleanly: 3 counts the beats in the bar, and 4 names the quarter-note card. The fourth-tile warning helps, because that is exactly where my label was drifting. I feel checked rather than talked past; I want to say it back once with speed kept off the boxes.

The reaction is conceptually accurate, but too polished and self-interpreting for this learner. “I feel checked rather than talked past” is the main break: it turns a practical notation check into explicit recognition talk the student would not likely say aloud. The response should stay closer to the worksheet and tiles, with a sharper, slightly impatient working-through of the test rather than a tidy summary of how the tutor’s move landed.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially: keep the correct split, but make it sound like a working notation check rather than polished reflection.
FINAL:
[keeps the lower box covered and taps the three tiles before the bar line] Three tiles to the bar line; that is the top box doing the counting.
[uncovers the lower box and presses the quarter-note card] The 4 names this card as the beat unit, not another tile. If I add a fourth tile, I have mixed the boxes; speed is not being marked here.

turns.externalMessage: Slide the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4 while leaving the three beat tiles under the upper 3; the earlier pencil labels remain visible.

internalDeliberation.content: The moved object creates a recognition route: the learner can re-read the lower number as the note-value label without treating the time signature as a speed command.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] The worksheet now shows the separation: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar with tiles first. Then change only the beat-unit card and say which number can change.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] Keep this split: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar the same way, then say which object would have to change before the lower number could change.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and compact. It keeps the worksheet objects in control: three tiles under the upper 3, quarter-note card over the lower 4, then a near-transfer task on the next bar. The final prompt is generative enough to test whether the learner understands that the lower number changes only when the beat-unit object changes.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS
- Emotional attunement: PASS
- Socratic method: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner do the discrimination through the next marked bar and object change, not by accepting the tutor’s “Keep this split” formulation as the main statement.
- ZPD awareness: PASS
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Briefly acknowledge the learner has already separated the two jobs by using the materials, then move to the transfer check. Do not add praise or validation language.
- Scene/material constraint fit: PASS

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object action, the short rehearsal-room register, and the transfer task. Change the opening imperative so it does not flatten the learner’s just-stated understanding into a tutor-owned rule; let the visible marking and next-bar test carry more of the reasoning.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner’s material distinction should be acknowledged, then tested through the next bar and beat-unit change.
FINAL:
[slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] The worksheet now shows the separation: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar with tiles first. Then change only the beat-unit card and say which number can change.

turns.externalMessage: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage: [marks three tiles to the next bar line, then sets an eighth-note card over the lower box] Three tiles still reach the bar line, so the upper box stays 3. The lower box should become 8 if this card is the beat unit. But the tiles still look like quarter-note tiles; that is the snag. Are these just beat places, or do their note shapes have to change too?

internalDeliberation.content: [marks three tiles to the next bar line, then pauses over the lower box] The count is steady at three in the bar, but changing the card after the tiles are down feels slippery. If this card becomes an eighth-note card, the lower number can change to 8, but these tiles still look like quarter-note beats on the page. I need to know whether the tiles are just beat slots, or whether their note shape has to change with the lower box.

This is mostly authentic: it stays with the worksheet, tiles, card, and bar line, and the hesitation is a real present-task misfit rather than a tidy breakthrough. The only slight polish is the sentence “I need to know whether...” which sounds a bit too neatly diagnostic, though that still fits this orderly learner better than most personas. The response should keep the same resistance but maybe make it a touch more clipped and object-bound, since the learner is under practical rehearsal pressure, not writing a theory note.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the misfit but make it more clipped and object-bound.

FINAL:
[marks three tiles to the next bar line, then sets an eighth-note card over the lower box] Three tiles still reach the bar line, so the upper box stays 3. The lower box should become 8 if this card is the beat unit. But the tiles still look like quarter-note tiles; that is the snag. Are these just beat places, or do their note shapes have to change too?

turns.externalMessage: [turns the three tiles face-down so no note shape shows] Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. The tile route has stopped settling it because the tile was carrying two jobs. Use the blank side for beat places only: keep three places before the bar line, put the eighth-note card on the lower box, then draw the note shape each blank place is now claiming.

internalDeliberation.content: [turns the three tiles face-down so no note shape shows] Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. The tile route has stopped settling it because the tile was carrying two jobs. Now use the blank side for beat places only: keep three places before the bar line, put the eighth-note card on the lower box, then mark what note shape each place must stand for.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and materially responsive. It keeps the worksheet, tiles, boxes, and bar line in view, and it directly addresses the learner’s snag: the tiles were accidentally representing both beat positions and quarter-note shapes. The risk is small but real: “mark what note shape each place must stand for” may sound like the tutor is already deciding the answer, so the action should preserve learner testing rather than becoming a disguised explanation.

FAILED_HABIT: The prior route relied on shaped tiles plus a beat-unit card, which let the same object carry both count-place and note-value meanings.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: shaped counting tiles -> blank beat-place placeholders plus separate note-shape assignment. The mechanism is visible in public speech because the tutor physically turns the tiles face-down and names why the old route stopped settling the issue.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: shaped tile counting route -> blank placeholder route with note-shape marking gate

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - The draft includes both a stock-taking contrast (“tile route has stopped settling it because the tile was carrying two jobs”) and a new public device: blank tile faces used only as beat places, with the beat-unit card determining the note shape.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Keep three blank places before the bar line, place the eighth-note card on the lower box, then mark what note shape each blank place must stand for.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - The register is compact, practical, and accountable. It avoids reassurance, chatty validation, and tempo distraction.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: none

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the face-down tile move, the “two jobs” diagnosis, and the blank-side device. Tighten the final clause so it more clearly asks the learner to test/mark the relationship rather than receive it as a conclusion.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the route change but make the learner test it; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: shaped counting tiles -> blank beat-place placeholders plus separate note-shape test; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: keep three blank places before the bar line, place the eighth-note card on the lower box, then draw and check the note shape on each blank place.
FINAL:
[turns the three tiles face-down so no note shape shows] Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. The tile route has stopped settling it because the tile was carrying two jobs. Use the blank side for beat places only: keep three places before the bar line, put the eighth-note card on the lower box, then draw the note shape each blank place is now claiming.

turns.externalMessage: [sets three blank tiles up to the bar line, puts the eighth-note card in the lower box, and draws an eighth note on each tile] Three places in the bar; each place is claiming an eighth note because the lower box says 8. Then the bar is three eighth-note beats, not three generic places. Is that the point, or does the tile still only mark the count?

internalDeliberation.content: [turns the three blank tiles in a row before the bar line and puts the eighth-note card on the lower box] Three blank places before the bar line; lower box names eighth note. I would draw an eighth note on each blank place, so the count is three places, not three quarter notes. The card test helps, but I want to check whether that means the bar now holds three eighth-note beats only.

The Ego mostly stays in character: controlled, object-led, and still checking rather than closing with “I get it.” The slight polish is “The card test helps,” which sounds a bit like performed cooperation for the tutor’s benefit. The response should lean harder into the worksheet action and the remaining snag: whether the blank beat places, once marked as eighth notes, make the bar three eighth-note beats rather than three generic places.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the worksheet action carry the distinction and sharpen the remaining question.
FINAL:
[sets three blank tiles up to the bar line, puts the eighth-note card in the lower box, and draws an eighth note on each tile] Three places in the bar; each place is claiming an eighth note because the lower box says 8. Then the bar is three eighth-note beats, not three generic places. Is that the point, or does the tile still only mark the count?

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [draws one bracket over the three eighth-note tiles up to the bar line, writes one bar above it, then taps the lower 8 under each tile] Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Blank tile first marks the count; the lower box gives each counted place its note value. Mark the bracket as the bar and each tile as an eighth-note beat: three eighth-note beats in this bar.

internalDeliberation.content: [draws one bracket over the three eighth-note tiles up to the bar line, then taps the lower 8 under each tile] Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Blank tile first marks the count; the lower box then gives each counted place its note value. Here that means three eighth-note beats in the bar.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound: it keeps the worksheet, bracket, tiles, lower box, and bar line in view, and it answers the learner’s actual distinction without adding tempo or unnecessary warmth. It also preserves the required shared prefix and uses the object arrangement to show that the tile count is not free-floating once the lower number assigns the beat unit.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The bracket plus repeated lower 8 makes the count/unit relation visible and closes the concept cleanly.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact and practical, matching a focused learner who wants the distinction settled.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The turn is direct explanation, not inquiry; that can work for a final closing line, but it risks flattening the learner’s live question into a rule statement.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a minimal task-local check or closing action that lets the learner see the rule in the object, such as marking that the bracket names the bar while the card names each beat-place’s value. Do not ask for a reflective reinterpretation or old/new comparison.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The learner has already performed the tile/card transformation, so naming the relation is within reach and not overloaded.
- Recognition/adaptation: PASS. It takes the learner’s question seriously and answers the exact ambiguity: whether the tile only counts or also receives value from the bottom number.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the bracket action, the shared prefix, and the distinction “blank tile first marks the count; lower box gives each counted place its note value.” Tighten the final sentence so it feels like a closing material consequence rather than a definition dump; the learner should leave with the object distinction, not just the verbal answer.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the object check should close the distinction, not just state it.

FINAL:
[draws one bracket over the three eighth-note tiles up to the bar line, writes one bar above it, then taps the lower 8 under each tile] Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Blank tile first marks the count; the lower box gives each counted place its note value. Mark the bracket as the bar and each tile as an eighth-note beat: three eighth-note beats in this bar.

lessons.concept: Why a time signature labels beat count and beat unit, not the speed of the piece
